does: This paper distinguishes machine interpreting as a specialized application of speech translation focused on live, real-time communication rather than textual fidelity alone. The authors identify and empirically demonstrate an accuracy illusion where systems achieving high benchmark scores fail to support effective interactive communication. They conduct a systematic analysis drawing on interpreting studies scholarship to uncover critical gaps between current machine interpreting systems and professional human interpreting practice. The paper consolidates findings into three core design priorities: agency (enabling context-sensitive initiative and conversational repair), grounding (incorporating multimodal and discourse-level awareness), and experience (supporting adaptive improvement through real interaction). This framework redefines evaluation criteria away from isolated translation metrics toward communicative effectiveness.
connects: This work builds on decades of interpreting studies research examining how professional interpreters manage dialogue flow, repair breakdowns, and maintain situational awareness through multimodal cues and discourse context. It responds to the growing gap between speech translation benchmark performance and practical usability observed across deployed systems, critiquing the dominance of BLEU-score style metrics in speech translation evaluation. The paper engages with human-computer interaction literature on grounding and collaborative communication, alongside work in dialogue systems and interactive machine translation that emphasize user agency and adaptive interaction. It synthesizes these previously disparate literatures to address why textually fluent but contextually impoverished translation systems fail in real communicative settings.
enables: This framework opens pathways for developing machine interpreting systems that move beyond isolated utterance translation toward supporting genuine interactive communication. It motivates new evaluation methodologies grounded in communicative success rather than lexical overlap, potentially redirecting how speech translation systems are benchmarked and developed. The three design priorities suggest concrete research directions: implementing context-sensitive repair mechanisms, integrating multimodal discourse understanding, and building systems that learn and improve through actual interaction rather than fixed training. This work could catalyze interdisciplinary collaboration between machine translation researchers, interpreting scholars, and HCI practitioners, ultimately enabling technologies that better support cross-lingual human-machine and human-human interaction in real-world settings.
